data.title: Visa: Conservative Guidance Creates Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Visa (NYSE:V) shares have been trading sideways since its June quarter report, despite very strong results with elevated growth and continued narrowing of the growth gap vs. Mastercard (MA). In my view, the main reason for the subdued stock reaction is a relatively cautious guide, which I argue was especially conservative due to tariff uncertainty and comp headwinds due to lapping last year's Olympics.

I estimate management left room for significant upside to expectations and see Visa trading at $400 a share by the end of 2026, reflecting significant upside.

Introduction To The Visa Investment Thesis

I've been covering Visa (and Mastercard) on Seeking Alpha for a few years now, maintaining a bullish view since initiation. My most recent article was in June.

I view Visa as an exceptionally unique business, which provides investors with a rare combination of a long runway for double-digit growth, low capital intensity, and an extremely wide moat. I don't know of any other company with such a low-risk profile, yet such an attractive growth trajectory.

Indeed, both Visa and Mastercard are long-time compounders and outperformers, though it's notable that Visa has been trailing its smaller counterpart. My explanation for the underperformance boils down to slower growth and a higher starting valuation. This is key, because as we look ahead, my preference for Visa is based on the fact that we are precisely in the opposite situation right now, as Visa is trading at a significant discount to Mastercard, while it's constantly narrowing the growth gap.

Looking ahead, I forecast Visa to grow 10%+ annually, with double-digit EPS growth driven by buybacks and gradual margin improvements.

Counterintuitively, I expect revenue growth to accelerate in the next few years, despite a growing scale, as Value-added Services and New Flows become a larger portion of total revenues, and those are growing much faster than consumer payments.

So, mid-teens growth for a wide-moat business—that's all investors' should ask for in a market-beating investment. Generally speaking, with this type of business, I tend to focus almost entirely on the long term, but I think investors can maximize their returns by increasing their position when near-term opportunities arise.

Best Growth Quarter In Years Overshadowed By Conservative Guide

Revenues in the June quarter were up 14% Y/Y, the fastest growth rate in years, led by 26% growth in Value-added Services, which is approaching a $12 billion run rate. Adjusted net income was up 19%, and adjusted EPS was up a remarkable 23%.

New Flows revenue was up 13%, and payments volume was up 8%, with related revenues growing faster, benefiting from price increases, higher FX volatility, and 12% growth in cross-border volume.

Near-Term Upside

Although revenue and EPS both came in significantly higher than expectations, earnings revisions for the year remained quite flat. This essentially means that all of the third-quarter beat is expected to be offset by a miss in Q4.

However, in reality, what I believe has happened is that consensus aligned itself with management's guidance, which was exceptionally prudent this time, to make up for tariff uncertainty, travel uncertainty, FX uncertainty, and tougher comps against last year's Olympics, which Visa sponsored.

Of note, management reiterated their previous guidance for FY25, i.e., low-double-digit revenue growth, which is roughly in line with consensus at 11%. That said, after growing more than 11% in the first three quarters, and assuming typical seasonality in Q4, I'd say we're more likely going to see 12% or higher. So, while Visa is a long-term story, I find the near-term setup especially favorable.

Visa's Valuation

At $351 a share, Visa trades at 31 times forward earnings and 27 times next year's estimates.

To me, this is right around fair valuation for Visa, and it's in line with its five-year average valuation. The thing is that even at fair valuation, a company growing earnings at a double-digit rate should yield market-beating returns. My price target for Visa is $400 a share, based on a 31x multiple on FY26 estimates, reflecting more than 14% upside in the near term.

Why I Prefer Visa Over Mastercard

Because Visa's fiscal year is one quarter ahead of the calendar year, the comparable multiples are 29x and 26x, based on calendar estimates. Meanwhile, Mastercard trades at 36x and 31x, reflecting 24% and 19% premiums, in line with the historical average.

To justify such a premium when we're talking about nearly identical businesses, we need to look to respective growth expectations.

In recent years, Mastercard outgrew Visa, in terms of total payments volume, by 3%-4%, but as we can see, this gap is narrowing, mainly because Visa is fending off market share losses due to incentives. Meanwhile, Visa is outgrowing Mastercard in Value-added Services and New Flows.

With the closing of the growth gap, I see increased probability for Visa's multiple expanding or Mastercard's multiple contracting. Either way, the way I see it, investing in Mastercard here comes with an additional risk that I don't want to take.

Also, fundamentally, I believe that Visa's larger scale will be a significant competitive advantage in Value-added Services and New Flows, and we're already seeing this carried out.

Conclusion

Visa provides investors with an unparalleled combination of reasonable valuation, a long runway for double-digit growth, low capital intensity, and a very wide moat.

With such long-term prospects, the near term isn't that important, but I think investors can maximize returns by exploiting favorable near-term setups. Such a setup is right now, as I expect Visa to meaningfully exceed subdued expectations in light of especially prudent guidance. I continue to favor Visa over Mastercard and reiterate the stock at a "Buy".

data.title: Prologis: Buy This Quality Industrial Warehouse REIT For Income And Growth
data.description:
I rate Prologis, Inc. (NYSE:PLD) a Buy, for income investors interested in long-term buy-and-hold real estate investment trusts (REITs). Prologis is the global leader in logistics real estate, which has most recently seen a boom in business due to online shopping. Amazon is their largest customer, followed by Home Depot and FedEx.

Given the size and scale of what they do, Prologis has a bright and long future ahead of it. The dividend history shows a consistently rising distribution, with growth rates similar to or better than funds created to capture dividend growth. Their balance sheet is solid, in my view, consistent with a large, well-run company with strong demand from world-class customers.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the REIT are:

  • Over the past year, PLD is down -13.43%.
  • Pays a quarterly dividend yielding 3.64%; goes ex-dividend on 9/16.
  • 5-year dividend growth rate of 12.16%.
  • #106 of the S&P 500.
  • Credit rating A2/A.
  • $205 billion in AUM, 73% domestic, 27% international.
  • Raised guidance for 2025, reporting Q3 on October 14.

Full disclosure, I’ve owned PLD in my portfolio as a core holding for many years, capturing both the growth of the logistics and warehousing business as the world moves to online shopping and the income from its consistent dividend. Recently, I’ve consolidated my REIT holdings into funds such as Cohen & Steers Real Estate Opportunities and Income Fund (RLTY) and Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI), both of which own PLD as one of their top 10 holdings.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Hold 3.33 (as of 9/4/25) due to the premium valuation. Both Wall Street and other Seeking Alpha analysts rate it as a Buy.

Global Industrial Warehouse REIT

Founded in 1983, Prologis Inc. is the global leader in logistics real estate with a focus on high-barrier, high-growth markets. As of June 30, 2025, the company owned or had investments in, on a wholly owned basis or through co-investment ventures, properties and development projects expected to total approximately 1.3 billion square feet (120 million square meters) in 20 countries. Prologis leases modern logistics facilities to a diverse base of approximately 6,500 customers principally across two major categories: business-to-business and retail/online fulfillment.

Q2 Earnings Results

On July 16, the company declared Q2 earnings, with the following financial highlights:

  • Core funds from operations (Core FFO) were $1.46 per share, up 9.0%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of $1.789 billion, up 4%.
  • Total available liquidity was approximately $7.1 billion.
  • Average occupancy ended the quarter at 95.1%.

Overall, the quarter exceeded the company’s expectations despite headwinds such as the impact of tariffs, which complicated operations. Despite the threats, Prologis’ customers were moving forward with plans to occupy more warehouse space, signing up for a record level of “build to suit” activity to cover future growth.

Performance To Date

PLD highlights their stellar history in a fact sheet on their website for investors. Core funds from operations (FFO) per share compound annual growth rate (CAGR) has a 5-year average of 12%, which surpasses other logistic REITs, the REIT average, and even that of the S&P 500. PLD’s dividend CAGR over the past five years is equally spectacular, in my view, posting a CAGR of 13%, which beats other REITs and the S&P 500.

$10,000 invested in PLD over the past 10 years would be worth $39,710 today, which well outperforms the sector as represented by Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ).

Valuation

PLD is priced at a premium with a forward price-to-adjusted FFO, or AFFO, of 24.56 compared to the sector median of 15.73, an increase of 56%.

Its total debt to equity is 61.59%, which is 37% below the sector median of 98%, which is positive. A lower debt-to-equity ratio provides greater confidence in challenging macroeconomic environments.

Since 2010, PLD has consistently increased the dividend each year to date.

Competition

In comparison to peers, as shown in the chart below, PLD is the largest at $105 billion in market capitalization. Its dividend, growth rate, and total returns are similar to competition.

Rexford Industrial Realty, Inc. (REXR) – offers the highest dividend yield of the group at 4.27% the highest 5-year dividend growth rate of 15.2%; however, one of the lowest 5-year total returns at -4.2%.

EastGroup Properties, Inc. (EGP) stands out for its highest 10-year total returns of 315%. I recently covered EGP in the article “EastGroup Properties Q4: Another Reason to Own This Industrial REIT.” Click on the article to read.

First Industrial Realty Trust Inc. (FR) – stands out for its high 5-year total return of 38.7% and the highest return on equity of 10.4%.

STAG Industrial Inc. (STAG) – has the second highest dividend yield at 4.1%. I recently covered the REIT with the article “STAG Industrial: Consistent Dividend Income from A Quality REIT.” Click on the article to read.

Over the past 10 years, PLD performed well vs. competition on a total return basis. Only EGP inched ahead in a tight race.

Outlook

During the Q2 Earnings call, management raised guidance for the rest of the year based on projected trends. Timothy D. Arndt, chief financial officer of Prologis said:

The increase in our guidance reflects our confidence in the strength and resilience of our business.

Clarity on tariff policy will help Prologis customers plan better, which will impact leasing and building activities. Overall, given the nature of their business, PLD is expected to be a perennial achiever driven by the growth of key customers such as Amazon, who will continually crave more warehouse space.

The next earnings report will be in mid-October.

Risks To Rating

The largest risk to PLD is a recession, which would slow down consumer spending. A slowing of demand would reduce the need for warehouse space and, in some cases, increase vacancies.

Bottom Line

I rate PLD a Buy for investors interested in growth and income, coming from REITs. PLD is one of the largest blue chip industrial warehouse REITs. It pays a consistent quarterly dividend distribution, which has a 5-year dividend growth rate of 12.16%. The company is well run, in my view, and has a solid balance sheet and strategy for growing the business. During the last earnings call, management raised guidance for the remainder of 2025, which reinforces my rating of Buy for the REIT.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: September Follies And GLTR, My Favorite Precious Metals ETF
data.description:
There is a Wall St. maxim that dictates, "September is the weakest month of the year." However, that Wall Street maxim is over-quoted and under-inspected. To wit:

The first US market index, the Dow, was created in 1896 and was composed of only 12 stocks, nine of which are no longer in business, and the remaining three of which are no longer components of the Dow!

So when some brokerage firm or newsletter writer intones that September is the worst month in which to invest, I submit you ask, "The worst? Since what beginning date?"

It was not until 1928 that the DJI added 18 new companies, making a total of 30, which is still the total today. However, most of these are not the same companies listed in the index today, either. Every year, the Dow Jones Industrial Average's primary owner and publisher, S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, shakes up the 30 to stay current with current trends.

In short, this index, like the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq, is a moving target! The indices are not static and, therefore, not a true basis for comparison.

Is it possible that some of the companies from back in the 1950s, or 1990s, or 2010s might have experienced a rough September for reasons no longer valid? Absolutely.

In 2025, September "looks" to be a great month, with a Fed interest rate cut seemingly a sure thing. This means many investors are now jumping into the market so they can own shares now, anticipating that the certainty of a rate cut will send the market wildly higher.

There is another Wall Street maxim I believe is more accurate: "Buy the rumor, sell the news." In other words, when people buy in anticipation of a positive event – like an interest rate cut – they may well find that once it happens, it is now old news and is already priced into the event. It is more typical that there will be one day or two that the market moves up, then backs down as if to say, "OK, that's done. Now what are you doing for me today?"

I think this may happen this month for two reasons. First, this is a cut signaled so far in advance and so strongly armed by President Trump (whose background in real estate leads him to believe interest rates must always be low) that the preponderance of buying has already occurred.

Second, rate cuts can actually be seen as a reverse indicator. If it appears that the Fed has had its arm twisted and is responding to the current political winds or whims, rather than its mandate to seek low inflation and high employment, then the cut will be questioned as if the Fed is no longer concerned about inflation. (Rate cuts tend to be inflationary.)

What To Do?

First, I think anyone buying long bonds today to "lock in" rates is buying a pig in a poke. I believe the value of these bonds will decline in real spending power as inflation is ignited by any future politically mandated rate cuts. The decline in value of the US dollar will accelerate if this occurs.

As I look closely at what international central banks are doing, it seems the smartest of them are buying fewer and fewer US dollars via US Treasury bonds. More and more, it is domestic individual investors who are buying these bonds for their "safety" and yield.

Second, these global bankers seem to be quietly leaving bond-buying to individuals and instead making a distinct move to physical stores of value: gold, silver, platinum, palladium, etc.

I don't think September will be a loser just because it is September. Like any other month, it might be up, it might be down. I am hopeful there will be a good rally that lasts and is the beginning of something bigger. But I am also a long-time student of the market, and I see it as just as likely that the "buy on the rumor, sell on the news" effect will predominate.

The Midas Touch

Either way, I believe a small position in precious metals vs. paper money will protect us as investors from serious declines in real purchasing power. I recently added shares of the Aberdeen Physical Precious Metals Basket Shares ETF (GLTR) to our Investor's Edge Growth & Value Portfolio.

Here were my short remarks to all our The Investor's Edge® subscribers in mid-August:

I previously took advantage of the rise in gold and silver to place some tight sell stops... Given that the S&P is now in the 98th percentile of price-to-earnings, I want a higher level of the stuff you can bite into (literally), stuff that does not tear easily nor float away in the presence of high winds.

GLTR is an easy ETF to research and discuss. I did not consider Price/Earnings, Price/Sales or the plethora of other characteristics I normally review. GLTR's top 10 holdings consist of just its top four holdings, and its top four holdings comprise the entire portfolio!

They are:

Physical Gold Bullion -- 65.12% of the portfolio

Physical Silver Bullion -- 27.01%

Physical Palladium Bullion -- 4.40%

Physical Platinum Bullion -- 3.47%

I like all four and believe the central banks beyond the US Fed are onto something important. I see gold, and the rest of the Big Four in precious metals, will serve us well as part of our portfolio that does not depend on what the rest of the market is doing.

Protective investing,

data.title: VOO Vs. JEPI: The Latter Is Likely To Outperform The S&P 500 In 2025
data.description:
VOO vs. JEPI: current valuation gap and VIX unlikely to last

We last covered the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:VOO) on July 16 with an article titled "VIG Vs. VOO: Best Time To Buy VIG In 10 Years.” The article rated VOO as a Hold given its unusual valuation premium relative to dividend growth stocks. As for the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI), our last work was published on July 21 in an article titled “JEPI ETF: Below-Average Income Potential Ahead.” That article also rated JEPI as a Hold given our outlook for muted volatility.

Since then, a few material developments have evolved around both ETFs and promoted this follow-up coverage. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top 2 developments on my list: A) the valuation divergence between these two funds and also B) our new outlook for market volatility given the latest geopolitical changes. In the end, these changes have led us to see a worsened reward/risk ratio for VOO and downgrade its rating to Sell. We maintain our Hold rating for JEPI because we believe it could play a hedging role against a correction in the S&P 500 (SP500).

Let me start with the first change: the valuation divergence between JEPI and VOO. As shown in the following chart, JPEI’s performance (in terms of total return) has closely tracked VOO’s after its inception in 2020. Then the divergence started in 2024, largely due to the takeoff of tech stocks, an area where VOO is concentrated more heavily than JEPI. Since our last writings on them, the divergence continued and further widened, as seen. All told, VOO enjoyed a total return of more than 100% since 2020, and JEPI “only” returned about 63% in this same period.

Next, I will argue that JPEI’s relative lag is likely to turn into a relative lead in the rest of 2025, given the current valuation gap between the SP500 and value-oriented stocks, and also our outlook for the S&P VIX Index (VIX).

JEPI and VOO: basic information

To better prime the subsequent analyses, let me highlight a few fundamental basics that are directly relevant. The VOO fund is a passive fund based on the S&P 500 index, as stated in its name. JPEI is an active fund also based on the S&P 500 index. As a result, JEPI only holds a subset of the stocks in the index (about 126 per its latest fund disclosure), and these stocks tend to be more value-oriented based on its fund description below (the emphases were added by me). In addition, JEPI also uses an option overlay strategy to generate high income and control portfolio volatility (emphasis added):

Seeks to generate high income through a combination of selling options and investing in U.S. large-cap stocks. Constructs a diversified, low volatility equity portfolio through proprietary research designed to identify overvalued and undervalued stocks with attractive risk/return characteristics. Seeks to deliver a significant portion of the returns associated with the S&P 500 Index with less volatility, in addition to monthly income.

Note that JPEI has a relatively short history, with an inception date in May 2020. In order to gain a broader historical perspective, some of our following discussions will rely on historical data from the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income Fund Class AA (JEPAX) fund, a mutual fund that uses a similar strategy as JEPI and provides historical data back to 2018.

JEPI vs. VOO ETF: closer examination of holdings

Now, let’s dive in and examine their holdings more closely. As aforementioned, both ETFs are based on the SP500 universe; thus, it should come as no surprise that their holdings overlap quite a bit. As you can see from the following pie charts, even among the top 10 holdings, there are several overlapping tickers, such as Nvidia, Microsoft Corp., Amazon, Alphabet Inc., and Meta.

Although there are also obvious differences. As seen, JPEI’s large position is an option position with a weight of 13.7% of its total assets. Second, as mentioned earlier, the essence of JPEI’s active management strategy is to actively buy stocks with more attractive valuations and sell those with less attractive valuations (the specifics are detailed in the following fund description, which was slightly edited with emphases added by me):

The fund advisers employ a 3-step process that combines research, valuation, and stock selection. The research allows the adviser to rank companies according to what it believes to be their relative value… The Fund buys and sells … using the research and valuation rankings as a basis. In general, the adviser selects securities that are identified as attractive and considers selling them when they appear less attractive.

The above value orientation is clearly illustrated by JPEI’s top holding, as shown below. For example, many of the fund’s tech holdings (such as MSFT, GOOG, and META) are less expensively valued than other tech holdings in VOO (such as AVGO and TSLA). Moreover, JEPI holds these tech stocks at a far lower concentration (around 1.5% to 1.6% each) than VOO. Finally, JEPI holds more stocks in more valuation-oriented sectors such as finance (V and MA), health care (ABBV), and consumer services (such as LOW).

All told, the above differences in exposure led to substantial differences in their sector exposure, as shown in the comparison below. As seen, some of the largest differences in exposure between the JEPI and VOO ETFs are Technology Services, Electronic Services, and health care. VOO has significant allocations to the first two sectors (21.75% and 23.42%, respectively), whereas JEPI holds far less. Within the healthcare sector, JEPI has an 11% weighting, which is noticeably above VOO’s 7.28%.

Under the current market conditions, JEPI’s above exposure makes better sense to us and is also better aligned with our own research. As an example, the following market dashboard shows our assessment of various market sectors. BTW, the dashboard is coded as a Google Sheet and is downloadable. As seen, the healthcare sector is currently one of the most attractively priced sectors in terms of dividend yield Z-score. It is also one of the sectors whose current valuation is least pressured by risk-free rates, judging by the Z-score of their dividend yield spread relative to 10-year treasury rates (US10Y). In contrast, the tech sector, where VOO is exposed disproportionately, is among the most expensively priced sectors by either metric.

Options, other risks, and final thoughts

As a downside risk for JEPI, its use of options (in the form of ELNs, Equity Linked Notes) can limit its upside in a rising market. As detailed in its fund description, the fund may “invest up to 20% of its net assets in ELNs, and the ELN would also reduce the Fund’s ability to fully profit from potential increases in the value of its equity portfolio”. Due to JEPI’s short history, as mentioned above, the chart below shows the issues using JEPAX historical data. As seen, JEPAX lagged VOO by a large margin in the past 5 years, a period where the SP500 enjoyed a secular bull run.

However, the use of options and/or ELNs can help generate high income and cushion drawdowns during market turbulence, as you can see from the historical data of JEPAX below. To wit, in the past ~5 years since JEPAX’s inception, the market suffered 2 corrections with magnitudes approaching 20%. In both cases, JEPAX suffered substantially less severe drawdowns.

Looking ahead, we see several catalysts that could help brighten JEPI’s relative performance besides the valuation gap discussed above. The current interest rate outlook points to dominant odds of rate cut(s) in the near future. Value-oriented stocks and income-oriented funds like JEPI have a strong bond-proxy nature and respond more sensitively and favorably to interest cuts from our experiences. In the meantime, we don’t expect the current low volatility to last for the broader equity market. The VIX index has been hovering below average for quite a few months already. Given the combination of elevated SP500 P/E ratios (especially among the growth stocks) and the ongoing geopolitical conflicts, we won’t be surprised to see the return of VIX, which could help boost JEPI’s income generation potential.

All told, this examination of JEPI and VOO is mainly triggered by 2 developments since our last analyses: their widened valuation gap and our updated outlook for VIX. These changes have led us to see increased odds of a correction for the broader market and consider JEPI as an effective hedging play. These considerations have led us to adjust our rating on VOO to sell and maintain our hold rating on JEPI.

data.title: 3 Reasons To Buy Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings
data.description:
When I last wrote about cruise operator Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NYSE:NCLH) in June, the stock wasn't in a good place. Not only was it trailing behind industry peers like Carnival Corporation (CCL) and Royal Caribbean Cruises (RCL), its drop was far bigger than even the consumer discretionary sector's already weak performance.

Cut to now, and NCLH is up by 35% since. To put it in perspective, this rise is a shade higher compared with other cruise stocks like (see chart below). Going by the 68% upside for the remainder of 2025 indicated by my estimates, then this should come as no surprise. However, at the time, I still had my reservations about whether it could actually materialize. There are good reasons for the rise, though, which even indicate that a further increase is possible.

3 Reasons to Buy NCLH

Before anything else, I'd like to point out that it's not as if the stock is immune to fluctuations even now. An example of this is the 4% drop in price in the two sessions following news that the company's president was stepping down last month.

However, by that time, the stock was able to absorb the drop as its fundamentals looked strong, especially with the company's Q2 2025 results, which resulted in over a 9% price increase on the day of release. This is a key reason for the stock's uptick, along with developments on the Hawaiian green tax front and its market multiples.

#1. Revenue growth picks back up

After the company's revenues declined by 2.9% YoY in Q1 2025, I had pointed out that the trend could continue in Q2 2025 as well, going by expectations for lower occupancy rates YoY.

But revenues surprised upwards, with a 6% YoY increase. While the occupancy rate in the latest quarter was still 103.9%, which was still lower than the 105.9% seen in Q2 2024, it did come in slightly higher than the company's projection of 103.2%.

Additionally, net yields, which is adjusted gross margin per capacity day, also surprised to the upside, with a 3.1% YoY growth rate compared to the expectation of 2.5%. This suggests the possibility that better pricing power could have also contributed to revenue growth.

#2. Hawaii's green tax is challenged

Besides weak revenue performance, another reason why NCLH was being punished back in June was the potential impact of Hawaii's green tax on the company. As it happens, its ship, Pride of America, is the only one that runs to the Hawaiian capital, Honolulu, through the year. It followed, then, that if any cruise company would be impacted by the tax, it would be Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings.

Even at that time, though, the extent of stock price weakness seemed like a stretch considering the Pride of America's likely small contribution to the company's financials. This in turn implied that over time, the stock can start inching up again.

Now, there's a question mark on whether the tax will actually be implemented, as the cruise industry's association, the Cruise Lines International Association [CLIA], has sued the state for the tax increase. It argues that no other state imposes such a tax and that the waters aren't owned by any one state. The tax, which could have been up to 14% as a result, now hangs in the balance.

#3. Profit outlook unchanged, but the valuations still look good

Next, even with the price rise in recent months, NCLH's valuation still looks good despite an unchanged outlook for adjusted EPS. The company has only increased its projection for occupancy rates marginally to 103% from the earlier 102.5%.

With the price rise and unchanged profit estimates, NCLH's forward non-GAAP P/E is now at 12.47x, up from the 9.13x the last I checked. But that doesn't make the stock unattractive now. It's still trading at a lower market valuation compared with the five-year average ratio of 15.92x. Further, it also compares well with the ratios for peers, with CCL at 15.67x and RCL at 22.67x. Even assuming that the stock can potentially go back up to its own past average implies some 28% upside to NCLH now.

What can go wrong

However, the stock's rise through the remainder of 2025 might not be entirely smooth, going by the fact that there's still risk to its revenues. In H1 2025, the company clocked some $4.65 billion in revenues. If it were to earn exactly the same amount in H2 2025 as well, the full-year figure would turn out to be $9.29 billion. This reflects a 2% decline for the full year.

However, it bears mentioning that this is a very conservative estimate. As per consensus estimates, the company's expected to see a 6% revenue increase, in line with the rise seen in Q2 2025. But it's still a risk worth keeping in mind given the weak results in Q1 2025. Also, Hawaii's tax might still go through; we don't know yet. If it does happen, NCLH can dip, especially compared with peers.

What next?

All in all, though, the outlook is positive for NCLH for now. Even if its sales underwhelm in 2025, they are balanced by the stock's strong market multiples. The earnings outlook might have remained unchanged, but even after the price increase in recent months, the stock is better placed than not just industry peers but also its own past average.

Sure, there could still be dampeners. Like cruise taxes. But even if they go through with it, the extent of the damage possible to the company's financials as a result will likely be limited. In any case, it remains to be seen whether the tax will actually go through or not. A bigger challenge can be another pullback in revenues, which is a risk to bear in mind. Even here, though, there's potential for upside too. I'm retaining a Buy rating on NCLH.

data.title: Miller Industries: Risks Don't Justify A Bullish Outlook (Downgrade)
data.description:
I have a very good track record in investing, but nobody has a perfect one. If they tell you that they do, they either just started and have a small sample size, or they are lying to you. I believe that confronting our mistakes helps us learn and grow, not only when it comes to investing but when it comes to all things in life. So even though it doesn't bring me any joy that my call on Miller Industries (NYSE:MLR) since I called it a "Buy" candidate in November 2024 has dropped 40.8% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 8.2%, I think it is instructive to look and see what is going on.

Based on the data provided, I see a business that is suffering from a major deficit in demand. This has caused revenue to plummet. And with that, profits and cash flows have also declined markedly. The one good thing here is that the overall net debt is quite low. Plus, the stock is not ridiculously priced thanks to the plunge that shares experienced. But until we see some additional clarity, I firmly believe that downgrading the company to a "Hold" is the appropriate choice.

Time to step back

If you are new to Miller Industries, it's good to know that the company operates as a producer of towing and recovery equipment. This is a business that has been around since 1990, and it sells its products through a network of distributors that serve all 50 U.S. states, as well as Canada, Mexico, and certain other foreign markets. If you were to judge the company based solely on its performance for the 2024 fiscal year compared to the 2023 fiscal year, you would be surprised that shares have fallen so much.

From 2023 to 2024, revenue for the company expanded from $1.15 billion to $1.26 billion. This increase in sales of 9% was driven mostly by higher production volume that management chalked up to supply chain stabilization and continued strong customer demand. Some of the increase was also thanks to a 3% average rise in pricing that the company was able to charge its customers.

The rise in revenue is accompanied by an increase in profitability as well. Net income for the company expanded from $58.3 million to $63.5 million. But that's not all. Operating cash flow grew from $11 million to $16.9 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $70.5 million to $79.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $92 million to $98.6 million.

As positive as the results during this time were, the picture changed significantly in 2025. For the first half of 2025, revenue for the company came in at only $439.7 million. That is 39% lower than the $721.3 million reported the previous year. Management said that this was caused by a reduction in chassis deliveries that the industry had to deal with because of inventory buildup in its distribution channel.

This is interesting to me. When the company announced financial results for the final quarter of its 2024 fiscal year, it provided in its annual report its opinion that challenges remained in this space. However, they said that they also expected to return to a "harmonized flow of manufactured equipment and chassis deliveries" throughout the current fiscal year. Admittedly, they did mention that the level of backlog might not have been a good indicator of future operating performance. Even so, that rhetoric seemed to point toward a picture that was neutral to positive for the company.

In the latest earnings call, however, management provided investors with a graph that showed chassis inventory levels compared to body inventory levels over an extended period of time. They showed, as is illustrated in the image above, that chassis inventory levels had now dropped below body inventory levels. This puts inventories right back to historical norms. However, management also said that body inventories are still elevated compared to optimal levels relative to demand. The fact that chassis inventories are currently below even that optimal level for body inventories indicates that things are moving in the right direction. But until body inventories also decline, there will most likely be some downside pressure for the business.

This drop in revenue brought with it a correspondingly large drop in profitability as well. Net income was slashed from $37.5 million to $16.5 million. Operating cash flow did improve from negative $1.7 million to positive $30 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $46.2 million to $27 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company contracted from $58 million to $29.2 million.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management believes that revenue will come in at between $750 million and $800 million. If we take the midpoint here, that would mean revenue for the second half of this year of $335.3 million. For context, for the same time last year, revenue was $536.2 million. This means a year-over-year drop in the second half of this year of 37.5%. That's not much better than the 39% decline seen during the first half of this year.

It is difficult to know what to expect when it comes to profits and cash flows. Management is not offering any guidance in this regard. And honestly, I understand why. With financial results so volatile already this year and worries about how long this inventory glut will take to clear up, bottom-line results could end up anywhere. We have already seen this year how bad a job management does at projecting results. When management announced results for the final quarter of 2024, they said that revenue this year would be between $950 million and $1 billion. Such a major downward revision in revenue already makes any sort of estimate on the bottom line completely unreliable.

If we project our results based on what we have seen so far for the year, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $46.7 million and EBITDA of $49.6 million. Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are low multiples in and of themselves, especially when we use the 2024 figures. For the 2025 estimates, the company looks much closer to being fairly valued. But it is admittedly teetering on the edge of that and being undervalued. Relative to other similar firms, however, the company is a bit pricey. In the table below, I compared it to five such enterprises. On a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis, three of the five firms ended up being cheaper. And when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, four of the five companies are cheaper than our target.

Company Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Miller Industries 10.4 10.3
Wabash National Corporation (WNC) 4.4 4.4
The Manitowoc Company (MTW) 25.1 7.6
Douglas Dynamics (PLOW) 16.8 14.5
Twin Disc, Incorporated (TWIN) 7.3 8.4
FreightCar America (RAIL) 8.6 6.6

Takeaway

I think there are reasons to be a bit cautious when it comes to Miller Industries. The company has underperformed this year compared to what I would have expected. Even management seems to have only a tenuous grip on industry conditions. This is certainly discouraging in and of itself. The good news is that, in addition to the industry showing early signs of stabilizing, the company is also taking some measures to improve its bottom line. On Aug. 14, for instance, management announced the elimination of 150 positions within the business. As horrible as this is for those affected, it will certainly help reduce the company's cost structure. But even with that factored in, I am not confident enough to rate this anything better than a "Hold" for now.

data.title: Credo Technology: Hold And Watch
data.description:
Shares of Credo Technology (NASDAQ:CRDO) have been on a tear in the last five months, and the recipe for success was strong general market and industry sentiment, coupled with the company’s exceptional execution.

In my previous article, which was published ahead of the fiscal Q4 2025 results, I noted that the strong share price momentum and strong top- and bottom-line growth trends, accompanied by a beat-and-raise quarter, could set the stage for continued gains.

Investors have gotten two strong beat-and-raise quarters since then, and the stock has more than doubled and remains in a strong uptrend. However, I would not be a buyer here, as both the valuation and the share price are extended, but other than potentially taking partial profits, I would not sell the stock either (if I had a position), as I still see the conditions as favorable for continued gains.

In other words, I have a hold rating on the stock with a watchful eye for negative sentiment and fundamental trend changes, and Credo has remained on Growth Stock Forum’s Momentum Ideas list (which I recently renamed from Trading Ideas) since early May.

Next-level execution in the last three quarters

Credo’s revenue growth hit an inflection point a few quarters ago, and so did the company's execution.

Management was generally conservative with guidance since going public, but the level of outperformance versus the guidance range has reached a new level since fiscal Q2 2025. The average revenue beat versus the midpoint of the guidance range went from approximately 1.6% to 11.1% in the last four quarters, and guidance for the next quarter was well ahead of the Street consensus every time.

Similarly, the average reported quarterly non-GAAP gross margin went from approximately flat versus the midpoint of the guidance range (granted, this was largely driven by one outlier quarter) to 210 basis points above the midpoint of the guidance range in the last four quarters.

The driver of the growth is, of course, the strong demand across the expanding customer base. In addition to the adoption of its AECs (Active Electrical Cables), Credo is also seeing trends toward “GPU and cluster densification” as another driver of the expanding total addressable market, with increased customer interest for AECs to expand from intra-rack solutions to rack-to-rack solutions. A big part of that is AECs being up to a thousand times more reliable while using half the power of optical solutions.

Importantly, Credo's reliance on Amazon as the key customer has also decreased significantly. Amazon accounted for as much as 84% of total revenues two quarters ago, and management noted on the earnings call that three hyperscaler customers contributed more than 10% of total revenue each in fiscal Q1 2026, with the split being 35%, 33%, and 20%. This still represents high customer concentration, but relying on three large customers is better than relying on just one.

Going forward, the company anticipates significant (but non-linear) year-over-year growth at a customer level with further diversification of the customer base. Management expects one more hyperscaler customer to exceed the 10% of revenue threshold this fiscal year.

The rise in beat-and-raise was also very strong. For the next fiscal quarter, Credo expects $230-240 million in total revenue versus the $198.5 million consensus ahead of the earnings report, and for fiscal 2026, management expects 120% growth over fiscal 2025 versus the 86% consensus growth estimate ahead of this week’s earnings report and the company's previous guidance for 85% Y/Y growth. The guidance is based on the current tariff regime, which remains uncertain and subject to change.

Based on the strong execution to date, I believe it is reasonable to expect fiscal 2026 revenues to exceed $1 billion, which would represent a Y/Y growth rate of at least 130%, with a bullish case of 140%+ Y/Y growth.

Overall, there are no reasons to complain about the performance of the business or the way the company is managing investor expectations.

The high price of strong growth

What investors or prospective investors can complain about is the valuation.

Credo is delivering exceptional growth, but there is currently a high price to pay for these growth rates. The price-to-sales ratio has expanded from the high teens six months ago to the mid-40s. YCharts did not add fiscal Q1 2026 results to the P/S ratio, and I calculated that the ratio has dropped to around 43 with the stock trading around $136 per share.

Credo can certainly grow into this valuation with the growth rates it is delivering, but even assuming next year’s revenues are $1.5 billion, the P/S ratio would only drop to the high teens.

Another thing to consider is that Credo's revenue growth rates have likely peaked or are about to in the next quarter or two. As such, while a P/S multiple expansion beyond the prior peak in the high 50s is possible, especially if market conditions reach new extremes, it does not appear likely.

Credo's valuation makes it vulnerable to large corrections even on in-line top- and bottom-line performance in the following quarters, and especially if they are coupled with worsening sentiment on high momentum and AI stocks.

For these reasons, I not only look at business performance but also observe share price trends, industry sentiment, and general market conditions. All three factors remain favorable for the time being, and as long as that is the case, Credo will remain on our Momentum Ideas list.

However, as I said in the introduction, the stock is now extended, and I define that as the price being significantly above its moving average lines—it is more than 20% above the 50-day moving average line (blue line in the chart) and more than 80% above its 200-day moving average line (red line in the chart).

Conclusion

Credo Technology's beat-and-raise performance in the last few quarters was much better than I expected, but so was its share price performance. With an extended share price and valuation, I rate it a hold and watch—hold for additional potential gains while keeping a close eye on the company's execution, industry sentiment, and share price trends.

data.title: Bicara Therapeutics: Promising, But Only For The Patient
data.description:
Shares of bifunctional antibody oncology concern Bicara Therapeutics Inc. (NASDAQ:BCAX) plunged 41% to $9.27 on May 23, 2025, after a data release from its sole clinical asset, ficerafusp alfa. The HPV-negative R/M HNSCC data were very strong but looked inferior to Merus’ (MRUS) bispecific antibody petosemtamab on initial inspection, which was released on the same day. With a pivotal trial enrolling patients for the same indication, data that suggest no winner should be declared yet, and trading at a small premium to cash, this small-cap merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Bicara Therapeutics, Inc., is a Boston-based clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focused on the development of "bifunctional" therapies for the treatment of solid tumors. The company has one clinical program, ficerafusp alfa (ficera), that is being advanced against epidermal growth factor receptor positive (EGFR+) solid tumors with an initial focus on head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC). Bicara was formed in 2018 and went public in September 2024, raising net proceeds of $336.9 million at $18 per share. After debuting as a hot IPO—first trade at $26.25. The stock currently trades right at $12.00 a share, with an approximate market capitalization of just north of $650 million.

Ficera

The company’s raison d'être is ficera, a bifunctional antibody designed to inhibit both EGFR and immunosuppressive transforming growth factor beta (TGF-β) signaling in the tumor microenvironment (TME) as a treatment for solid cancers. EGFR is a cell-surface tyrosine kinase growth factor receptor involved in many tumor-promoting pathways, whose overexpression is associated with squamous cell tumors such as HNSCC, cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC), squamous cancer of the anal canal (SCAC), and colorectal cancer (CRC). TGF-β is a cytokine molecule that is central to the body’s early development and maintenance during maturity. However, in the TME, it promotes tumor growth and helps cancer cells evade immune surveillance and removal. The isoform TGF-β1 is predominantly expressed in squamous cell cancers.

The initial target of ficera is HPV-negative, recurrent/metastatic (R/M) HNSCC. Overall, HNSCC represents ~67,000 new domestic cancer cases annually (4% of total) with a grave five-year survival rate of 13%. Approximately 90% of head and neck cancers are squamous cell, 10% are metastatic on initial diagnosis, with another 30% recurring or metastasizing after initial treatment. In total, there are 23,000 R/M HNSCC cases in the U.S. every year with a median overall survival (MOS) of 12 months. If that isn’t bad enough, 80% of R/M HNSCC cases are HPV-negative—usually a function of smoking or chewing tobacco—a status associated with significantly worse outcomes.

The current standard of care is Merck’s mega-blockbuster PD-L1 inhibitor Keytruda (pembrolizumab) with or without chemotherapy, which is contingent upon tumor and TME PD-L1 expression. Keytruda monotherapy produced an objective response rate (ORR) of 19% and median progression-free survival (mPFS) of 3.2 months in its registrational trial for HNSCC. In patients with little-to-no PD-L1 expression, Eli Lilly’s (LLY) anti-EGFR Erbitux (cetuximab) and chemotherapy are the standard of care, with even lower response and survival rates. With these poor outcomes and clearly high unmet need, global sales of HNSCC meds totaled $4.0 billion in 2024.

Bicara believes the ineffectiveness of the current treatments is the result of elevated TGF-β1 in the TME and that its inhibition will promote improved anti-tumor activity for both EGFR inhibitors and anti-PD-L1 therapies, translating to more durable responses and longer survival.

To that end, the company has entered ficera into a potentially registrational, placebo-controlled Phase 2/3 trial (FORTIFI-HN01) that is enrolling ~650 patients with a PD-L1 combined positive score—a measure of PD-L1 prevalence—greater than or equal to one, no prior systemic therapy in an R/M setting, and no HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. Essentially, the participants will be randomized 1:1:1 to receive one of two Keytruda-ficera combination doses or Keytruda monotherapy in a first-line (1L) setting, with all the qualifiers powering the trial to produce data worthy of an accelerated approval. Two interim analyses will be performed: one to determine optimal dosage (n=60) and the other to determine ORR within six months of follow-up (n=415). The primary endpoint is OS of the optimal dose combination versus Keytruda monotherapy. Management is hoping for an ORR interim readout in 2027.

That said, the market’s response to interim data from ficera’s Phase 1/1b study was far short of receptive, even though the combination delivered a 54% confirmed ORR (64% unconfirmed ORR, 18/28), with a complete response (CR) rate of 21% (6/28) and a disease control rate (DCR) of 89% (25/28), all in the tough-to-treat HPV-negative population. Eighty percent (12/15) of the confirmed responses were categorized as deep (>80% tumor reduction). That cohort also demonstrated a median duration of response (mDOR) of 21.7 months and mPFS of 9.9 months, with 48% progression-free at 12 months. mOS was 21.3 months with an OS rate at 12 months of 62% and 46% at 24 months, which compared favorably to Keytruda, which demonstrated an mOS of ~9 months and an OS rate at 24 months of 20%-25%.

Normally, given the very poor outcomes from the current treatment paradigm, that data set would have inspired a meaningful rally. Instead, shares of BCAX cratered 41% to $9.27 in the subsequent trading session on May 23, 2025.

The culprit came in the form of data from Dutch pharma Merus’ bispecific (EGFR x LGR5) antibody petosemtamab, also in combination with Keytruda for seemingly the same indication, which was released on the same day. In a Phase 2 trial, that combo demonstrated a confirmed ORR of 63% (27/43), comprised of six CRs (14%) and 21 partial responses (PRs). mPFS was nine months, with mDOR and mOS not yet reached. The OS rate at 12 months was 79% (30/49). It was the OS rate at 12 months that both sell-side analysts and the market focused on, declaring petosemtamab the winner over ficera, with shares of MRUS up 33% in the trading session and BCAX plummeting. They have continued to surge, now up nearly 60% since the readout.

However, the results were not derived from homogenous patient populations, with Merus data including eight HPV-positive patients, of which 50% (4/8) had an ORR. And given the depth of response and the better CR profile, Bicara is confident that its OS rate at 24 months will carry the day, a data point not yet provided by Merus.

In addition to HNSCC, the company is advancing ficera against CSCC, SCAC, and CRC in earlier-stage trials and is contemplating a move into locally advanced HNSCC, which represents another 60% of the HNSCC population on initial diagnosis. The most promising indication that has produced data is SCAC, where ficera plus Keytruda demonstrated an ORR of 25% (7/28) versus an 11% ORR for Keytruda monotherapy.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

To advance ficera, Bicara held cash and equivalents of $436.6 million on June 30, 2025, providing it an operating cash runway into H1'29. The company burned through just over $25 million worth of cash to support all operations in Q2.

Since second-quarter results were posted on August 12th, analyst firms have reissued 5 Buy and 1 Hold ratings on BCAX. On average, the prognosticators expect ficera to be approved in 2028, providing revenue of $482 million in FY30 and $939 million in FY31. Obviously, forecasts this far out should be taken with a large grain of salt.

Verdict:

Given the sample sizes, the non-homogeneous populations in their respective trials—although removing the HPV-positive data might strengthen Merus’ Phase 2 HPV-negative data—and the deep responses in the ficera trial (80% with >80% tumor clearance versus less than 50% for petosemtamab), this is far from a slam-dunk case regarding which candidate is superior. That said, petosemtamab is further along in the clinic, with its registrational trial data due in 2026, and it will likely enjoy first-mover advantage if both therapies are approved in combination with Keytruda in a 1L setting. The other issue at hand is the extreme length of clinical trials for cancer, with first revenue from Bicara not coming until 2029 under a "most likely" scenario.

On the flip side, ficera will continue to produce combination data for other indications in its earlier-stage trials, including CRC, an indication with FY24 worldwide sales of $7.1 billion. It is trading at a ~$220 million ($4 per share) premium to cash, which is cheap given the data from ficera to date.

That said, the company is years away from any potential commercialization and is not projected to be profitable in this decade. Therefore, the risk-reward profile makes BCAX stock appear fairly valued at this time.

data.title: LSI Industries: Turning The Lights On
data.description:
In April, I believed that shares of LSI Industries Inc. (NASDAQ:LYTS) were enlightened, as the business has seen strong growth, driven by both organic and acquisition activities. Its integrated lighting and display solutions serve a wide range of industries and applications, with more room to expand into adjacent markets.

Momentum accelerated in the fourth quarter, both in terms of sales and also margins, the latter of which were pressured during the year. All this makes me upbeat here, as shares have moved up for the right reasons, and trade at reasonable multiples, with the fundamental performance backing up the move higher.

Lighting & Display Solutions

LSI is a vertically integrated provider of both indoor and outdoor lighting and display solutions. The vertical integration includes design, production, and installation of such solutions, a key distinguishing feature.

Such applications are found in QSR restaurants, gas stations, retail & grocery outlets, parking, sports complexes, and warehouses. However, the demand for such solutions is increasing and is seen in other applications as well, relating to security, infrastructure, and others.

Over the past decade, the company has grown its business from about $300 million in sales to about half a billion, but the bigger achievement is that operating margins, which typically fluctuated around break-even levels in the 2010s, are now around 7% of sales. The company has further ambitions, targeting sales up to $800 million in 2028, with further margin expansion targeted as well.

Valuation Thoughts

In August 2024, LSI reported a 6% fall in full-year sales to $470 million, with GAAP operating profits of $35 million and change translating into margins equal to 7% and change in relation to sales. GAAP earnings of $25 million came in at $0.83 per share, and while adjusted earnings came in at $0.95 per share, much of the reconciliation was the result of stock-based compensation (SBC) expenses, something which I am not happy to adjust for.

EBITDA was reported at $51 million, for a leverage ratio of 1.0 times amidst a similar net debt load, which in itself came from a similar valued acquisition of EMI Industries in April 2024. This deal was set to add $87 million in sales, creating a boost to the 2025 results.

Struggles

After posting a 12% increase in first-quarter sales for 2025 and another 36% increase in second-quarter sales, momentum appeared strong. Notably, the acceleration in the second quarter was driven by the broken-up merger between Kroger (KR) and Albertson's (ACI), resulting in catch-up demand. However, earnings have been lagging somewhat.

Trading at $16 in April, the company's equity was valued at $480 million, for an enterprise valuation of just over half a billion, with earnings power seen just below a dollar per share on a $600 million revenue base. This resulted in a less than 1 times sales multiple and market multiple in terms of earnings, as the company announced another acquisition.

In March, the company acquired Best Holdings in a $24 million cash deal, with the acquisition set to contribute $24 million in profitable sales, with EBITDA margins seen at 16%. Pegging earnings potential around a dollar per share, with margin expansion having the potential to drive earnings growth in the future, I was leaning upbeat and willing to keep track of the developments from here.

Moving Ahead - Momentum Is Unleashed

So, in between the spring of this year and today, shares have risen some 50%, with shares having gradually recovered to the $20 mark over the summer, accelerating to $23 in recent weeks.

In April, the company announced a 22% increase in third-quarter sales, with adjusted earnings down another four cents to $0.20 per share. These earnings year-to-date are down eight cents to $0.72 per share, which is somewhat disappointing.

In August, fiscal fourth quarter sales were reported up 20% to $155 million, yet adjusted earnings were up 6 cents to $0.34 per share. All this made the full-year sales up 22% to $573 million. GAAP operating profits for the year rose by a percent to $35 million, yet amortization expenses rose by about a million to nearly $6 million. Consequently, adjusted earnings were flat (as the definition was not exactly the same as in 2024) at $1.07 per share, with GAAP earnings down 4 cents to $0.79 per share.

Net debt has come down to $45 million, and with EBITDA up to $55 million, leverage ratios have come down to 0.8 times already. That is comforting, as the performance was solid from a growth perspective, yet full-year adjusted EBITDA margins fell by 140 basis points to 9.6% of sales. On the bright side, fourth-quarter margins of 11.0% were actually up ten basis points from the year before.

Important to see is that full-year organic growth came in at 4%, but 11% in the fourth quarter, as the real question is what margins can look like. If the company can grow EBITDA margins by a point, that could be worth about $0.15 per share, as fourth quarter momentum continues into 2026, it could create a real roadmap for realistic earnings just over a dollar per share, or perhaps even more.

In fact, realistic earnings power trend around $1.25 per share based on the fourth quarter numbers, which has been driving the shares for the right reasons, as I have been too cautious in the spring. Based on such a number, a $23 stock trades at a market multiple here, as leverage is rapidly coming down, leaving more financial room for future M&A.

And Now?

Frankly, I kick myself for missing the LYTS rally, but the market at large has seen a similar rally since the spring. The rally in the case of LSI has been backed up by a very strong fourth quarter set of results, which is encouraging and means that much of the move is backed up by operating momentum in the business.

This makes me upbeat about the business here. However, at the same time, I am a bit cautious about chasing LYTS shares here as well, making me still very interested to keep track of the developments from here.

data.title: Essex Property Trust: California Exposure A Tailwind In A Weak Rental Market
data.description:
Shares of Essex Property Trust (NYSE:ESS) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing 13% of their value. Elevated rates have weighed on real estate valuations, and the apartment sector has faced particular pressure, given concerns about weakening rent trends. I last covered shares of Essex in June, rating the stock a “Buy”, which has been a poor recommendation given the 7% decline in the stock after a disappointing earnings release. With updated financials and recent underperformance, now is a good time to revisit Essex.

Rents surged in the aftermath of COVID, leading to an increase in supply, which has then brought rents back down. You can see the dramatic whiplash of the past several years below. Now, over 2024 and into 2025, rents were getting steadily less negative, and I expected rents to flip back positive this year. Normalized rent levels and much higher financing costs have reduced new construction, after all, which should remove excess supply pressure. However, over the past few months, rents have begun turning back lower, dashing hopes for a 2025 recovery.

This downturn has been surprising and a significant headwind for shares in the sector. Notably, it is not occurring due to any changes in the supply picture, which is pretty well entrenched (and highly forecastable in the near term, given it takes 12-24 months to complete a property). As you can see, supply is steadily falling, and I expect this decline to continue until next year before likely bottoming out. All else equal, the supply side is a net tailwind for rent growth.

Rather than supply pressure, it appears we are seeing the demand side weaken, likely as elevated absolute rent levels become harder for renters to afford, especially with job growth slowing and wage growth fairly muted. The cooling jobs market is constraining household budgets, limiting how much landlords can raise rents. Frankly, it is difficult to see an acceleration in demand growth at this stage of the economic cycle. Eventually, as supply continues to fall, I expect rents to stabilize even in this muted demand environment. However, that is now likely a 2026 phenomenon, and we may not see a return to more normal ~3% rent growth until 2028.

That said, it is important to emphasize that real estate trends are local. As you can see below, the South is generally performing much worse than the Northeast, as that area has more supply and affordability pressures. Essex has about 40% of units in Northern California, 40% in Southern California, and 20% in Seattle. San Francisco is currently the hottest market in America, though Los Angeles and San Diego are softening. Its geographic positioning is a reason I expect Essex’s financials to outperform the sector.

Indeed, we have seen Essex’s results hold up fairly well. In the company’s second quarter, reported on July 29, Essex Property Trust generated $4.03 of funds from operations (FFO), which beat estimates by $0.05. FFO was up 2.3% from last year. Same-property net operating income (NOI) growth was 3.3%, with revenue growth of 3.2%. Lower property tax costs in Washington state were also a benefit. In Q2, same-store rent was up 3%. The company is benefiting from tech companies finally pulling workers back into offices.

Similar to what we are seeing in national statistic aggregators, Essex reported that Northern California and Seattle are outperforming, with Los Angeles the laggard. San Francisco revenue was up 6.5% from last year, whereas Los Angeles was up just 2.8%. LA was also sequentially flat, despite the fact that Q2 tends to be seasonally stronger. This speaks to the softening management is seeing. Los Angeles has more supply and is seeing higher delinquencies. Overall, Essex reported a strong 96.2% occupancy, flat to a year ago.

While Northern California has been strong, there are definite concerns about the sustainability of this recovery. Insofar as AI causes job losses, many traditional technology jobs could be automated away, with some legacy software companies potentially facing threats from new AI offerings. A slowing in the tech jobs market would likely disproportionately impact the Bay Area and curtail this nascent recovery. This cautionary point is notable with management expecting rents to decelerate in H2, as its revised guidance implies about 2.7% H2 rent growth from 3% previously.

The guidance changes the company made were not as strong as they first seemed, in my view. Alongside results, management raised the midpoint of FFO guidance by $0.10 to $15.91, with same-store NOI expected to rise 3.1% from 2.7%. However, there is nuance here. While the midpoint was raised, Essex actually brought down the high end of guidance. In my view, Essex’s low end was previously extremely conservative, enabling management to bring up the low end a fair bit. Markets paid more attention to the curtailed high-end than the boosted low-end as shares sold off post earnings, which is reasonable in my view.

These guidance changes reflect the fact that the first half of the year has been solid but that management is more tepid about the outlook over the rest of the year. This tepidness, combined with weakening national rent trends, has been a challenging combination for the stock. One additional headwind is that, besides operating its own business, Essex has a structured finance platform where it generally provides “mezzanine” financing. This means it is in the middle of the capital structure (debt is at the top and common equity is at the bottom), generally via instruments like preferred stock and subordinate debt. At one point, the portfolio reached $700 million, accounting for 9% of FFO. Management aims to bring this down to 3% via maturities later this year. A major reason for this is that capital markets access has improved, meaning the ~10% coupon it used to earn is rolling down to 5%, as noted on the earnings call. At that rate, Essex can generate better returns by developing and buying new properties.

Importantly, in this period of uncertainty, Essex has a strong financial position. It has $6.8 billion of debt for a ~52% debt-to-capital ratio. That translates to a 5.5x debt-to-EBITDA leverage ratio, below the 6x I like to see. This balance sheet provides flexibility to consider property acquisitions if attractive opportunities arise. I also view its 3.9% yield as highly secure and poised for moderate ongoing dividend growth.

Back in June, I was targeting $16 of FFO this year. I still believe Essex is well-positioned to withstand weaker national trends, given its geographic positioning and some benefit from tech companies being slower but now more insistent on workers being in the office. I now see the company generating $15.90-$16.00 in FFO this year. Shares now offer a ~6.1% FFO yield. This matches the FFO yield of a company like Mid-America Apartments (MAA), which has far more exposure to the Sun Belt, the center of current weakness.

As such, I view Essex as offering an attractive valuation given its safer geographic footprint and strong balance sheet. While my $320 price target is likely not attainable in this more cautious backdrop, as Essex’s results show it to have better rental trends than peers, I expect the stock to recover losses and push back to the $290-$300 range. Accordingly, I would use the recent dip to "Buy" and add exposure to a high-quality operator.
